title: MeshNL
description: Multi-stage hierarchical MeSH classification trained on the full PubMed baseline. Stage 1 fine-tunes BiomedBERT for 15-branch prediction (macro recall >0.95); Stage 2 fine-tunes a BioLORD dual encoder on 3M paper-term pairs with hard negative mining for fine-grained term retrieval (Recall@50). Engineered a streaming MeSH XML parser and Colab training infrastructure with mid-epoch checkpointing.

blog.subtitle: Two-stage retrieval for a vocabulary where the tail matters

sections.heading: Why MeSH classification is hard
paragraphs: PubMed tags every paper in its database with MeSH descriptors, a controlled vocabulary of around thirty thousand terms organized into a tree. A zero-shot encoder maps adjacent concepts to similar-sounding but wrong terms, and on rare descriptors (anything outside the high-frequency head of the distribution like Neoplasms or Humans) it performs poorly. The problem with a mis-tagged paper isn't that it's obviously wrong, it's that it's subtly wrong in a way a reviewer won't catch, and the error propagates into any downstream analysis that uses MeSH for filtering.

sections.heading: Two-stage design
paragraphs: The system is a two-stage pipeline because a single dense retrieval model over all thirty thousand terms is expensive and unnecessary. Stage 1 is a multi-label classifier with fifteen sigmoid outputs, one per MeSH branch, using BiomedBERT as the backbone and a loss reweighted by inverse class frequency so rare branches receive proportional gradient. This stage's only job is to narrow the candidate set from thirty thousand to a few hundred per paper, making Stage 2 tractable. Stage 2 is a dual encoder: query tower BiomedBERT, term tower BioLORD, trained with InfoNCE loss over three million paper-term pairs at temperature 0.05.

sections.heading: Hard negative mining
paragraphs: Standard InfoNCE with random negatives doesn't produce a model that distinguishes between near-neighbor terms, which is exactly the failure mode that matters for rare tail terms. Every N batches, the training loop pulls the top-K false positives for each query from a nearest-neighbor lookup over the full descriptor embedding cache and adds them as explicit negatives in the next step. The cache is rebuilt after each update cycle so the hard negatives stay current as the model improves. BioLORD was frozen for the first few epochs and then unfrozen so both towers could co-adapt without the term tower drifting early in training.

sections.heading: Training infrastructure
paragraphs: Training ran on Colab, which required solving three problems that a managed training environment doesn't have. The streaming MeSH XML parser reads the full release file without loading it into memory, yielding each descriptor as it parses and writing to Parquet for the training pipeline. Checkpointing writes optimizer state to Google Drive every N batches (not just weights), so a disconnect costs forty minutes of compute instead of ten hours. The whole setup is more fragile than a proper training cluster, but the cost difference is significant enough that it was the right tradeoff.

sections.heading: Evaluation
paragraphs: The final evaluation compares BM25, frozen BioLORD, and the fine-tuned dual encoder on the same held-out set. BM25 performs worst across all branches. Frozen BioLORD is competitive on common branches and collapses on the tail. The fine-tuned model wins across all branches, with the largest gap on rare terms, which are the terms the training was actually designed to improve. Recall at 50 on held-out papers is 2.2 times zero-shot baseline.
